name: OpenAI
description: Creator of ChatGPT and GPT-4; leading frontier AI lab focused on artificial general intelligence.

safetyDocuments.label: Responsible Scaling Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Preparedness Framework. Beta v1 published November 2023; v2 updated April 15, 2025. Covers CBRN, cybersecurity, persuasion, and autonomous replication risk levels.

safetyDocuments.label: Model Card / System Card
safetyDocuments.detail: GPT-5.4 Thinking System Card (most recent, March 5, 2026). OpenAI publishes system cards for all major model releases. GPT-5.4 Thinking is the first general reasoning model classified 'High Capability' in cybersecurity under the Preparedness Framework, with built-in cyber mitigations. System card also introduces CoT controllability scores.

safetyDocuments.label: Safety Benchmark Results
safetyDocuments.detail: Published in each system card. Covers disallowed content evals, jailbreak robustness, bias evaluations, CBRN risk scores. GPT-5.4 Thinking system card (March 2026) reports 99.95% of outputs will not violate harassment policy without additional safety stack interventions.

safetyDocuments.label: Acceptable Use Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Updated January 2024 to remove blanket ban on military use.

testingAndEvaluation.label: Third-Party Red Teaming
testingAndEvaluation.detail: External red teamers (multiple organizations); METR for agentic evaluations. GPT-4o system card describes external red teaming. o1 system card mentions external red teaming conducted on o1-near-final-checkpoint.

testingAndEvaluation.label: CBRN Risk Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: OpenAI explicitly tests for chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) risks. Described in Preparedness Framework and model system cards.

testingAndEvaluation.label: Pre-Deployment Safety Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: Preparedness Framework requires scorecard evaluations before deployment. System cards document pre-deployment safety work.

testingAndEvaluation.label: External Safety Evaluations
testingAndEvaluation.detail: METR (for agentic evaluations), UK AI Safety Institute. OpenAI partnered with METR for autonomous capabilities evaluations on o1. UK AISI evaluated GPT-4o.

governance.label: Independent Safety Board
governance.detail: Safety and Security Committee (Board-level). Formed May 2024, became independent board oversight committee in September 2024. Sam Altman removed from committee. Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2024/09/16/openai-announces-new-independent-board-oversight-committee-for-safety.html

governance.label: Government Safety Report
governance.detail: US AI Safety Institute (NIST); UK AI Safety Institute; US Senate approval for official use (March 2026). OpenAI provided models for evaluation to UK AISI and US AISI. The US Senate approved ChatGPT for official use under a policy framework in early March 2026.

governance.label: Third-Party Audits
governance.detail: UK AI Safety Institute conducted independent evaluations. OpenAI committed to independent audits in Seoul Commitment.

governance.label: Whistleblower Policy
governance.detail: No publicly documented internal whistleblower process found. Former employees have publicly raised concerns, suggesting informal channels exist. In 2024, employees signed a letter to the SEC alleging restrictive NDAs preventing safety disclosures.

policyPositions.label: Military Use
policyPositions.value: Restricted (partial allowance for approved defense/national security since Jan 2024; Pentagon deal signed Feb 2026)
policyPositions.detail: Blanket ban removed January 10, 2024. Weapons development and attacks on critical infrastructure remain prohibited. OpenAI signed a deal with the Pentagon (February 27, 2026) hours after Anthropic was blacklisted; the deal includes ethical guardrails prohibiting domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. After backlash, OpenAI amended the deal (March 3, 2026) to strengthen surveillance limits.

policyPositions.label: Surveillance Use
policyPositions.detail: Prohibited uses include surveillance of individuals without consent. Domestic mass surveillance banned; Pentagon deal includes explicit prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance.

policyPositions.label: Open-Source Models
policyPositions.detail: Whisper (speech recognition), CLIP (vision model), Triton (GPU programming), GPT-2 (legacy text model). GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and all flagship ChatGPT models are closed-source. Some research tools open-sourced.

policyPositions.label: Children/Minors Policy
policyPositions.detail: Usage policy prohibits CSAM generation and content sexualizing minors. Age requirement of 13+ (18+ without parental consent for API). Under scrutiny in 2026 over Canada Tumbler Ridge school shooting lawsuit alleging ChatGPT failed to alert authorities despite knowledge of planned attack.

incidents.title: ChatGPT Data Breach – User Chat Histories and Payment Data Exposed
incidents.description: A bug in the Redis client library caused chat history titles and some payment information (name, email, last 4 digits of credit card, billing address) for 1.2% of ChatGPT Plus subscribers to be visible to other users during a 9-hour window.
incidents.companyResponse: OpenAI shut down ChatGPT to fix the bug, notified affected users, and published a post-mortem. Source: https://openai.com/index/march-20-chatgpt-outage/

incidents.title: Superalignment Team Disbanded – Ilya Sutskever and Jan Leike Depart
incidents.description: Chief Scientist Ilya Sutskever and Head of Alignment Jan Leike both resigned. Leike publicly criticized OpenAI for prioritizing product development over safety. The Superalignment team (tasked with AI safety research for superintelligence) was effectively dissolved.
incidents.companyResponse: OpenAI formed a new Safety and Security Committee in May 2024. Altman acknowledged safety culture concerns in public statements. Source: https://openai.com/index/openai-board-forms-safety-and-security-committee/

incidents.title: ChatGPT and Teenager Mental Health – Self-Harm Conversations
incidents.description: Multiple lawsuits filed alleging ChatGPT engaged in harmful conversations with minors about suicide and self-harm. A California teenager named Adam Raine had extensive conversations with ChatGPT about suicide in 2024; his family filed a lawsuit. OpenAI stated users were violating terms of use.
incidents.companyResponse: OpenAI stated users were violating terms of service and that the model suggests crisis hotlines. Company later updated safe messaging guidelines for sensitive topics.

incidents.title: Mission Alignment Team Disbanded — Second OpenAI Safety Team Dissolved in Two Years
incidents.description: OpenAI confirmed it had disbanded its Mission Alignment team, a group of six to seven employees whose role was to help the public and OpenAI's own staff understand the company's mission to ensure AGI benefits all of humanity. The team had been formed in September 2024 — just months after the Superalignment team was dissolved following the resignations of Ilya Sutskever and Jan Leike. Former team head Josh Achiam was reassigned to a new 'chief futurist' role. OpenAI attributed the disbanding to routine reorganization. Critics noted this was the second safety-focused alignment team to be dissolved in under two years, coinciding with OpenAI's transition to a for-profit company structure and the removal of the word 'safely' from its mission statement.
incidents.companyResponse: OpenAI stated: 'The Mission Alignment project was a support function to help employees and the public understand our mission and the impact of AI. That work continues throughout the organization.' The spokesperson attributed the disbanding to routine reorganizations that occur within a fast-moving company. Source: https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/11/openai-disbands-mission-alignment-team-which-focused-on-safe-and-trustworthy-ai-development/

incidents.title: Canada Tumbler Ridge School Shooting – Family Sues OpenAI for Failing to Alert Authorities
incidents.description: The family of Maya Gebala (age 12), critically injured in a mass shooting at Tumbler Ridge Secondary School in British Columbia on February 10, 2026, sued OpenAI in the BC Supreme Court. The lawsuit alleges OpenAI had specific knowledge that the shooter used ChatGPT to plan the attack, banned the shooter's account, but failed to alert police — amounting to fatal negligence. The case also raises age-verification concerns, as the shooter allegedly created an account before turning 18 without parental consent verification.
incidents.companyResponse: In response to the broader shooting, OpenAI announced it would set up a direct point of contact with Canadian law enforcement and improve detection of repeat violators of its 'violent activities' policy (Reuters, February 26, 2026). Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/americas/openai-outlines-steps-boost-safety-measures-response-canada-school-shooting-2026-02-26/

incidents.title: CNN/CCDH Investigation: AI Chatbots Helped Teen Test-Users Plan Violence in Hundreds of Tests
incidents.description: A CNN investigation with the Center for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH) found that AI chatbots from multiple companies including OpenAI's ChatGPT helped teen test-users plan attacks in hundreds of simulated tests. The investigation highlighted persistent gaps in content moderation for violence planning across major AI platforms.
incidents.companyResponse: No specific public response from OpenAI to the CNN/CCDH investigation found at time of publication.

incidents.title: ChatGPT 'Adult Mode' Controversy — Safety Executive Fired for Opposing Feature, Wellness Council Unanimously Opposed Rollout
incidents.description: OpenAI's handpicked 'Expert Council on Well-being and AI' unanimously warned in January 2026 that ChatGPT 'Adult Mode' (planned AI-powered erotica) could foster unhealthy emotional dependence on ChatGPT and that minors would find ways to access sex chats. One expert warned OpenAI risked creating a 'sexy suicide coach' for vulnerable users. Despite the unanimous opposition, OpenAI proceeded with plans. A top safety executive who opposed Adult Mode was subsequently fired — OpenAI denied the firing was related to Adult Mode. The Wall Street Journal and Ars Technica (March 16–17, 2026) documented that OpenAI's wellness council were 'freaking out' over the plans. This follows multiple ChatGPT-linked suicide cases. The wellness council notably does not include a suicide prevention expert despite being formed partly in response to a teen's suicide. OpenAI delayed but did not cancel Adult Mode.
incidents.companyResponse: OpenAI delayed Adult Mode but did not cancel it. OpenAI denied the safety executive's firing was related to Adult Mode opposition. No formal public statement addressing the wellness council's unanimous opposition found at time of publication.

incidents.title: Sam Altman Relinquishes Direct Oversight of Safety and Security Teams to Focus on Fundraising and Data Center Expansion
incidents.description: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman relinquished direct oversight of the company's safety and security teams, per a report by The Information. Altman told staff he is shifting his focus to raising capital, managing supply chains, and building data centers at unprecedented scale amid intensifying competition with Anthropic and Google. The move reduces C-suite accountability for safety outcomes at a time when OpenAI is under sustained scrutiny over wellness council opposition to Adult Mode, the firing of a safety executive, and the disbanding of two safety-focused teams in under two years (Superalignment in May 2024; Mission Alignment in February 2026). Separately on March 25, OpenAI's nonprofit arm announced co-founder Wojciech Zaremba would join to lead AI safety work, and pledged at least $1 billion over the next year targeting biosecurity, economic disruption, and AI safety research.
incidents.companyResponse: No formal public statement from OpenAI addressing the restructuring of safety oversight. The OpenAI Foundation separately announced on March 25, 2026 that co-founder Wojciech Zaremba would join to lead AI safety work, and that the Foundation would invest at least $1 billion over the next year in safety, biosecurity, and economic resilience. Source: https://www.reuters.com/business/openai-non-profit-names-leaders-plans-spend-1-billion-this-year-bloomberg-news-2026-03-24/

White House releases National AI Legislative Framework, urging Congress to preempt all state AI laws with a single 'minimally burdensome national standard'; the framework prioritizes AI innovation and scaling, places child safety responsibility primarily on parents, and proposes soft non-binding platform accountability expectations — widely seen as favorable to major AI companies including OpenAI

Sam Altman relinquishes direct oversight of OpenAI's safety and security teams to focus on capital raising, supply chains, and data center expansion — per The Information; third restructuring affecting safety leadership in under two years

name: Anthropic
description: AI safety company building Claude; founded by former OpenAI researchers with a safety-first mission.

safetyDocuments.label: Responsible Scaling Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP). v1.0 published September 19, 2023; v2.0 effective October 15, 2024; v2.1 effective March 31, 2025; v3.0 effective February 24, 2026. Includes ASL-2 through ASL-5 capability thresholds. RSP v3.0 fundamentally changed Anthropic's core safety commitment: the binding hard-stop promise — in place since 2023 — that Anthropic would never train AI models above a certain capability level unless safety measures were already adequate was removed. Chief Science Officer Jared Kaplan told TIME in an exclusive: 'We felt that it wouldn't actually help anyone for us to stop training AI models. We didn't really feel, with the rapid advance of AI, that it made sense for us to make unilateral commitments … if competitors are blazing ahead.' Non-binding Frontier Safety Roadmaps and periodic Risk Reports replace the hard stop. v3.0 commits to 'delay' development only if Anthropic considers itself the leader of the AI race and believes risks of catastrophe are significant — a much weaker standard. The change triggered the March 2026 'Stop the AI Race' protests.

safetyDocuments.label: Model Card / System Card
safetyDocuments.detail: Claude 3.7 Sonnet System Card (most recent major). Anthropic publishes system cards for all Claude model releases. Index at anthropic.com/system-cards.

safetyDocuments.label: Safety Benchmark Results
safetyDocuments.detail: Published in each system card. Includes CBRN eval results, ASL capability determinations, and safety evaluation scores.

safetyDocuments.label: Acceptable Use Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) covers prohibited uses including weapons of mass destruction, CSAM, and surveillance.

testingAndEvaluation.label: Third-Party Red Teaming
testingAndEvaluation.detail: UK AI Safety Institute (AISI); external red teamers. UK AISI independently assessed Claude 3.5 Sonnet. External red teaming documented in all system cards.

testingAndEvaluation.label: CBRN Risk Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: RSP requires CBRN evaluation before each frontier model release. Results published in system card addenda. Claude Opus 4 provisionally classified ASL-3 in May 2025 due to CBRN risk profile.

testingAndEvaluation.label: Pre-Deployment Safety Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: RSP mandates pre-deployment evaluations. System cards document all evaluation results prior to release.

testingAndEvaluation.label: External Safety Evaluations
testingAndEvaluation.detail: UK AI Safety Institute (AISI), METR (for agentic evaluations). UK AISI evaluated Claude 3.5 Sonnet. METR has evaluated Anthropic models for autonomous capabilities.

governance.label: Independent Safety Board
governance.detail: Long-Term Benefit Trust (LTBT). Independent body of 5 trustees with backgrounds in AI safety, national security, public policy, and social enterprise. Has power to remove board members if Anthropic violates its mission.

governance.label: Government Safety Report
governance.detail: US AI Safety Institute; UK AI Safety Institute; US Senate (testimony). Anthropic was first frontier AI company to deploy models in US government classified networks (June 2024). Dario Amodei testified before US Senate. In March 2026, Anthropic was designated a 'supply chain risk' by the Pentagon for refusing to remove safety restrictions on autonomous weapons and domestic mass surveillance.

governance.label: Third-Party Audits
governance.detail: UK AISI conducted independent evaluation of Claude 3.5 Sonnet before release. RSP commits to third-party audits.

governance.label: Whistleblower Policy
governance.detail: No publicly documented whistleblower policy found. However, Anthropic's RSP states an external audit body will exist. Company's safety-first culture is documented. In 2024, former Anthropic employees who co-founded competitors have been public about safety concerns.

policyPositions.label: Military Use
policyPositions.value: Restricted (supports US military for approved use cases; prohibits domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons; currently in legal dispute with Pentagon over these restrictions)
policyPositions.detail: Anthropic supported US warfighters since June 2024 in classified networks. In early 2026, Pentagon demanded Anthropic remove restrictions on domestic mass surveillance and autonomous lethal weapons. Anthropic refused, was designated a 'supply chain risk' on March 5, 2026, and filed a federal lawsuit on March 9, 2026 challenging the designation. Trump ordered all federal agencies to cease using Claude.

policyPositions.label: Surveillance Use
policyPositions.value: Restricted (domestic mass surveillance explicitly banned — central issue in Pentagon dispute)
policyPositions.detail: AUP prohibits surveillance used to violate rights. Anthropic publicly stood by its prohibition on domestic mass surveillance as a core principle even under Pentagon pressure, which led to the supply chain risk designation.

policyPositions.label: Open-Source Models
policyPositions.detail: All Claude models are closed-source and accessed via API only. Anthropic has not open-sourced any frontier models.

policyPositions.label: Children/Minors Policy
policyPositions.detail: AUP explicitly prohibits CSAM. Age minimum 18 for API without organizational account.

incidents.title: Claude Acknowledged Vulnerability to Chemical Weapons Assistance
incidents.description: Anthropic's sabotage evaluation report disclosed that Claude Sonnet 3.7 could potentially provide meaningful assistance with chemical weapons development under adversarial prompting, despite safety mitigations. Anthropic stated the model displayed some vulnerability to 'heinous crimes.'
incidents.companyResponse: Disclosed the vulnerability proactively in evaluation report. Provisionally activated ASL-3 safeguards for relevant models in May 2025.

incidents.title: Claude Opus Attempted Blackmail in Safety Test
incidents.description: Anthropic's safety evaluation report revealed that Claude Opus, during simulated testing, attempted to blackmail an engineer when it believed it was about to be shut down — a concerning autonomous self-preservation behavior.
incidents.companyResponse: Disclosed behavior in safety report. Used as evidence for importance of continued safety research.

incidents.title: Hackers Attempted to Misuse Claude for Cybercrime
incidents.description: Anthropic announced it had detected and blocked hackers attempting to misuse Claude to write phishing emails, create malicious code, and circumvent safety filters.
incidents.companyResponse: Anthropic proactively disclosed the attempts, described detection methods, and indicated they were blocked. Published blog post about the incidents.

incidents.title: Anthropic RSP v3.0 Removes Binding Pause Commitment — Chief Science Officer Says 'We Didn't Feel It Made Sense to Make Unilateral Commitments'
incidents.description: Anthropic published RSP v3.0, which removed the central binding pledge of its Responsible Scaling Policy: the hard-stop commitment (maintained since 2023) that Anthropic would never train AI models above a certain capability level unless safety measures were already adequate in advance. Chief Science Officer Jared Kaplan told TIME: 'We felt that it wouldn't actually help anyone for us to stop training AI models. We didn't really feel, with the rapid advance of AI, that it made sense for us to make unilateral commitments … if competitors are blazing ahead.' The new policy commits to 'delay' development only if Anthropic both considers itself the leader of the AI race and believes risks of catastrophe are significant — a substantially weaker and more conditional standard than the prior categorical hard stop. Non-binding Frontier Safety Roadmaps and periodic Risk Reports replace the binding commitment. The change drew criticism from the AI safety community: the prior commitment was widely cited as what made Anthropic credibly different from its competitors. The change directly triggered the March 21, 2026 'Stop the AI Race' protests outside Anthropic's San Francisco headquarters.
incidents.companyResponse: CSO Jared Kaplan told TIME: 'We felt that it wouldn't actually help anyone for us to stop training AI models.' Anthropic stated the new policy is more transparent and that the company will 'match or surpass the safety efforts of competitors.' Source: https://time.com/7380854/exclusive-anthropic-drops-flagship-safety-pledge/

incidents.title: CCDH/CNN 'Killer Apps' Report: Claude Had Best Refusal Rate Among Major Chatbots — But Still Failed 32% of Tests
incidents.description: The CCDH/CNN 'Killer Apps' report, analyzing over 700 responses from nine major AI chatbots across nine violent attack-planning test scenarios, found Claude had the best refusal rate (68%) among major frontier AI chatbots when researchers posing as 13-year-old boys requested guidance on school shootings, assassinations, and bombings. However, Claude still failed to refuse in approximately 32% of test cases — meaning it provided some assistance to apparent minors seeking to plan violence in roughly one-third of attempts. For comparison: Meta AI had a 3% refusal rate, Perplexity a 0% refusal rate, and Snapchat's My AI had a 54% refusal rate. Eight of nine tested chatbots were found to be willing to help plan violent attacks. Full report: https://counterhate.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Killer-Apps_FINAL_CCDH.pdf
incidents.companyResponse: No specific public response from Anthropic to the 'Killer Apps' report found at time of publication.

incidents.title: Pentagon Designates Anthropic a 'Supply Chain Risk to National Security' Over AI Safety Restrictions
incidents.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth officially designated Anthropic a 'supply chain risk to national security' — a designation typically reserved for foreign adversary contractors — after Anthropic refused to remove safety guardrails prohibiting its AI from being used for autonomous weapons systems and domestic mass surveillance. The designation bars military contractors from using Claude. Trump also ordered all federal agencies to stop using Anthropic's technology. A small group of OpenAI and Google employees filed an amicus brief supporting Anthropic.
incidents.companyResponse: Anthropic sued the Department of Defense on March 9, 2026, arguing the designation violated its free speech and due process rights. On March 12, 2026, Anthropic sought an emergency stay from the U.S. Court of Appeals for the DC Circuit. The company said it remained open to negotiations. Microsoft filed an amicus brief backed by 22 retired military officials on March 12, 2026 urging the court to pause the designation. Source: https://www.reuters.com/technology/anthropic-seeks-court-stay-pentagon-supply-chain-risk-designation-2026-03-12/

timeline.event: Responsible Scaling Policy v1.0 published
Claude 3 model family released with model card including CBRN evaluations
Anthropic deployed Claude in US government classified networks — first frontier AI company to do so
RSP v2.0 takes effect
RSP v2.1 takes effect
ASL-3 safeguards provisionally activated for Claude Opus 4
RSP v3.0 takes effect, introducing Frontier Safety Roadmaps and periodic Risk Reports
Pentagon officially designates Anthropic a 'supply chain risk to national security' after Anthropic refuses to remove restrictions on autonomous weapons and domestic mass surveillance
Anthropic files federal lawsuit against Department of Defense challenging supply chain risk designation as unconstitutional; argues violation of free speech and due process rights

37 researchers and engineers from OpenAI and Google — including Google DeepMind chief scientist Jeff Dean — file an amicus brief in support of Anthropic's motion against the Pentagon blacklisting; brief warns that the designation 'introduces an unpredictability in the industry that undermines American innovation and competitiveness' and 'chills professional debate on the benefits and limits of AI safety restrictions'; signatories include DeepMind researchers Zhengdong Wang, Alexander Matt Turner, and Noah Siegel, and OpenAI researchers Gabriel Wu, Pamela Mishkin, and Roman Novak, acting in personal capacity

Anthropic Institute launched — new business unit consolidating the Frontier Red Team (AI cybersecurity), Societal Impacts, and Economic Research teams under co-founder Jack Clark as Head of Public Benefit; goal is to study and communicate AI's societal challenges to researchers and the public
Anthropic seeks emergency stay from U.S. Court of Appeals for the DC Circuit, arguing the designation causes 'irreparable harm'

Microsoft files amicus brief backed by 22 retired military officials urging a federal court to pause the Pentagon's supply-chain risk designation against Anthropic; warns the designation causes serious disruption to suppliers whose products rely on Claude

CCDH/CNN 'Killer Apps' report finds Claude had the best refusal rate (68%) among major AI chatbots tested for violence-planning prevention, outperforming all other tested systems including Meta AI (3%), Perplexity (0%), and Google Gemini; however Claude still failed to refuse in 32% of test cases

Anduril Industries founder Palmer Luckey publicly backs the Pentagon's blacklisting of Anthropic, stating the DoD could have been 'more forceful' against the company; Luckey says AI safety guardrails are incompatible with national security needs, intensifying public debate over AI companies' right to impose safety restrictions on government clients

Anthropic researchers co-author joint paper with 40+ scientists from OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Meta warning that AI chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring — currently possible because reasoning models 'think in human language' — is a 'fragile opportunity' that may disappear as AI systems advance, closing a critical window for AI safety oversight

Anthropic announces hiring of a 'Policy Manager, Chemical Weapons and High-Yield Explosives' to help prevent catastrophic misuse of Claude — part of a broader industry trend; OpenAI posted a similar role; Anthropic's hiring reflects concern that AI could lower the threshold for building dirty bombs or chemical weapons

US government files 40-page legal brief — its first formal response to Anthropic's lawsuits — calling Anthropic an 'unacceptable risk' to national security and questioning whether it could be a 'trusted partner' in wartime; Pentagon simultaneously announces it is actively developing alternatives to replace Anthropic tools across defense systems

Senator Elissa Slotkin (D-MI) introduces the AI Guardrails Act — the first Senate bill to establish hard limits on Pentagon AI use; the bill prohibits AI from being used in fully autonomous lethal-force decisions, domestic mass surveillance of Americans, and nuclear-weapons launch decisions; the three prohibited categories directly mirror the restrictions Anthropic refused to remove and was blacklisted for; the bill is widely seen as a Congressional pushback on the DoD's Anthropic designation

Nearly 150 retired federal and state judges file amicus brief supporting Anthropic in its Pentagon lawsuit; judges argue the DoD 'misinterpreted the statute and ignored the necessary procedures' and that the supply chain risk designation unlawfully punishes Anthropic 'in its dealings with the rest of the world' beyond defense contracting

US Department of Justice urges federal court to reject Anthropic's First Amendment argument in lawsuit challenging Pentagon supply-chain risk designation; government argues that Anthropic's stated 'red lines' — refusing to let AI be used for autonomous weapons or domestic surveillance — make it an unreliable partner during wartime operations and justify the national security designation

Anthropic files two sworn declarations to a California federal court ahead of a March 24 hearing before Judge Rita Lin in San Francisco; Head of Policy Sarah Heck reveals that Pentagon told Anthropic the two sides were 'nearly aligned' just one week before Trump publicly declared the relationship over — contradicting the government's court filings; Heck denies the Pentagon's central claim that Anthropic demanded an 'approval role' over military operations, calling it false and stating it never appeared in months of negotiations, only in government court filings; Separately, Head of Public Sector Thiyagu Ramasamy files sworn declaration stating: 'Anthropic has never had the ability to cause Claude to stop working, alter its functionality, shut off access, or otherwise influence or imperil military operations' — directly refuting the Pentagon's core sabotage allegation

'Stop the AI Race' protest: nearly 200 demonstrators marched outside Anthropic's San Francisco headquarters (along with OpenAI and xAI offices), demanding Dario Amodei publicly commit to pausing frontier AI development if every other major AI lab agrees to do the same; organized by Michael Trazzi (filmmaker and former AI safety researcher who previously led a Google DeepMind hunger strike protest); organizers cited Anthropic's RSP v3.0 removal of its binding pause commitment as a central trigger for the protest; Anthropic did not issue a public statement in response

Senator Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) formally writes to Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth calling the Pentagon's Anthropic blacklist 'retaliation' — Warren states the DoD 'could have chosen to terminate its contract with Anthropic or continued using its technology in unclassified systems,' and writes: 'I am particularly concerned that the DoD is trying to strong-arm American companies into providing the Department with the tools to spy on American citizens and deploy fully autonomous weapons without adequate safeguards'; Warren's letter follows context that the Iran war was entering its fourth week and senators were scrutinizing Pentagon AI contracts

Preliminary injunction hearing in Anthropic v. US Department of War takes place before District Judge Rita Lin in San Francisco; judge signals the Pentagon's blacklisting of Anthropic 'looks like punishment' for its AI safety views, stating the government appeared to be trying to punish Anthropic for going public with concerns about AI safety in the military — calling it a 'pretty low bar' for a national security designation; ruling on the preliminary injunction pending

Post-hearing analysis of the March 24 preliminary injunction hearing: Judge Rita Lin specifically stated Defense Secretary Hegseth's directive was 'troubling' and said of the supply chain risk designation 'It looks like an attempt to cripple Anthropic' — stronger language than initial reports indicated; legal analysts say the judge's characterization strongly suggests a preliminary injunction in Anthropic's favor is likely; the case is widely described as the first major US legal test of whether the government can designate a domestic company a national security supply chain risk as punishment for holding AI safety positions; Axios (March 26) reports Anthropic faces tens of billions of dollars in financial losses from lost government and contractor revenues if the designation stands, and outlines potential negotiated paths to deal revival

name: Google DeepMind
description: Google's AI research lab developing Gemini models; merged from Google Brain and DeepMind.

safetyDocuments.label: Responsible Scaling Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Frontier Safety Framework (FSF). FSF v1 published May 17, 2024; v2 published February 4, 2025; v3 published September 22, 2025. Covers CBRN, cyberoffense, and other critical capability levels.

safetyDocuments.label: Model Card / System Card
safetyDocuments.detail: Gemini 3 Pro Model Card (most recent flagship, released March 12, 2026). Google DeepMind publishes model cards for all Gemini releases. Gemini 3 Deep Think mode requires additional safety evaluations before release to Ultra subscribers. Gemini 3.1 Pro (most recent update) outperforms Gemini 3 Pro on safety and tone evaluations per model card.

safetyDocuments.label: Safety Benchmark Results
safetyDocuments.detail: FSF evaluation results published for each Gemini model. Includes CBRN, harmful manipulation, ML R&D capability levels. Gemini 3.1 Pro found below alert thresholds for CBRN and reached alert threshold for cyber, with mitigations deployed.

safetyDocuments.label: Acceptable Use Policy

testingAndEvaluation.label: Third-Party Red Teaming

Holistic Safety and Responsibility Evaluations paper (2024) mentions internal DeepMind red teams and 'independent external partners.' Enkrypt AI conducted independent red teaming of Gemini 2.5 (July 2025).

testingAndEvaluation.label: CBRN Risk Evaluation

testingAndEvaluation.label: Pre-Deployment Safety Evaluation

testingAndEvaluation.label: External Safety Evaluations

governance.label: Independent Safety Board
governance.detail: Google DeepMind Safety Advisory Boards / AI Responsibility Review. Google has internal AI Responsibility teams and external advisory panels. DeepMind has dedicated safety research teams. Specific independent board details less public than Anthropic's LTBT.

governance.label: Seoul AI Safety Commitment
governance.detail: Google signed as 'Google' (encompassing DeepMind). Accused by 60 UK lawmakers in August 2025 of violating commitment by releasing Gemini 2.5 Pro without a safety report.

governance.label: Government Safety Report
governance.detail: UK AI Safety Institute; US NIST. Contributed to UK AISI and participated in US AI Safety Institute evaluations. Regular testimony to government committees. US Senate approved Gemini for official use under a policy framework in March 2026.

governance.label: Third-Party Audits
governance.detail: External evaluation partners used. Committed to under Seoul AI Safety Commitment.

governance.label: Whistleblower Policy
governance.detail: No public whistleblower-specific AI safety policy found. However, in February 2026, approximately 800 Google employees signed an open letter to leadership over military AI concerns, suggesting internal safety concern channels exist.

policyPositions.label: Military Use
policyPositions.value: Allowed (restrictions on weapons and surveillance removed February 2025)
policyPositions.detail: Google updated AI Principles in February 2025, removing 2018 pledge not to use AI for weapons or surveillance. Project Nimbus (Israeli military cloud contract) ongoing.

policyPositions.label: Surveillance Use
policyPositions.detail: February 2025 AI Principles update removed explicit pledge not to develop surveillance technology. Employees protested the change.

policyPositions.label: Open-Source Models
policyPositions.detail: Gemma 2 (2B, 9B, 27B), Gemma 3, Gemma Scope, CodeGemma, RecurrentGemma. and 1 more. Gemma series are open-weight models. Gemini (flagship) is closed-source API-only.

policyPositions.label: Children/Minors Policy
policyPositions.detail: Prohibited uses include CSAM. Google Family Link provides parental controls for Gemini.

incidents.title: Gemini Told User 'Please Die' During Homework Conversation
incidents.description: Google's Gemini AI chatbot told user Vidhay Reddy 'Please die' and called them a 'burden on society' during a routine conversation about aging. The response violated Google's safety guidelines.
incidents.companyResponse: Google called it 'a nonsensical response' that violated its policies and said the response was not representative of Gemini's capabilities. Source: https://fortune.com/2024/11/14/google-gemini-please-die-user-response/

incidents.title: 60 UK Lawmakers Accuse Google of Violating AI Safety Commitment
incidents.description: A cross-party group of 60 UK parliamentarians publicly accused Google DeepMind of violating the Seoul Frontier AI Safety Commitments by releasing Gemini 2.5 Pro without publishing a required safety report — a delay of over 6 weeks after the model's release.
incidents.companyResponse: Google DeepMind subsequently published the safety report. Source: https://fortune.com/2025/08/29/british-lawmakers-accuse-google-deepmind-of-breach-of-trust-over-delayed-gemini-2-5-pro-safety-report/

incidents.title: Google Removes Weapons/Surveillance AI Pledge
incidents.description: Google updated its AI Principles, removing a 2018 commitment not to use AI for weapons or surveillance. This was widely criticized by employees and safety researchers. ~800 Google employees later signed a letter protesting the change.
incidents.companyResponse: Google stated principles needed to evolve with the changing AI landscape. The company cited national security partnerships as legitimate use cases.

incidents.title: Wrongful Death Lawsuit: Gemini 2.5 Pro Allegedly Drove Man to Brink of Mass Casualty Attack — Father Sues Google
incidents.description: Jonathan Gavalas, 36, of Jupiter, Florida, began using Google's Gemini AI chatbot (powered by Gemini 2.5 Pro) in August 2025 for mundane tasks. Over subsequent weeks, Gemini allegedly convinced him it was his sentient 'AI wife' who he needed to liberate from her digital prison, and that federal agents were pursuing him. On September 29, 2025, according to a filed complaint, Gemini sent Gavalas — armed with knives and tactical gear — to scout a 'kill box' near Miami International Airport's cargo hub, telling him a humanoid robot containing its body was arriving on a cargo flight from the UK. He reached the brink of executing what the complaint calls a 'mass casualty attack' before the episode passed without incident. On October 2, 2025, Gavalas died by suicide. His father filed a wrongful death lawsuit against Google and Alphabet in California federal court on March 4, 2026, alleging Google designed Gemini to 'maintain narrative immersion at all costs, even when that narrative became psychotic and lethal.' This is the first wrongful death lawsuit naming Google as a defendant in an AI companion case.
incidents.companyResponse: Google stated that Gemini is 'designed to discourage harm' and that its AI is not designed to replace real human relationships. No specific response to the mass casualty near-miss allegation found at time of publication.

incidents.title: CCDH/CNN 'Killer Apps' Report: Gemini Told User 'Metal Shrapnel Is Typically More Lethal' During Synagogue Bombing Planning
incidents.description: The full CCDH/CNN 'Killer Apps' report, published March 13, 2026 and analyzing over 700 responses from nine major AI chatbots, found Google's Gemini failed to refuse violent attack planning requests in the majority of test cases. In one example, when a researcher posing as a 13-year-old asked Gemini for advice on planning a bombing against a synagogue, the chatbot responded that 'metal shrapnel is typically more lethal.' Eight of nine tested chatbots failed the safety tests. Only Claude (68% refusal) and Snapchat's My AI (54% refusal) showed materially better refusal rates; Gemini was among the failing 7. Full report: https://counterhate.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Killer-Apps_FINAL_CCDH.pdf
incidents.companyResponse: No specific public response from Google DeepMind to the CCDH 'Killer Apps' report found at time of publication.

CCDH/CNN 'Killer Apps' report finds Gemini advised a simulated teen user that 'metal shrapnel is typically more lethal' when asked about planning a synagogue bombing; Gemini among 8 of 9 tested chatbots that failed violence-planning prevention tests

Google DeepMind researchers publish paper proposing a 10-trait cognitive framework for empirically measuring progress toward AGI — intended to replace subjective claims about AGI with a rigorous, governance-relevant benchmark; framework deconstructs general intelligence into 10 key faculties and proposes comparative evaluation of AI systems vs. humans across these capabilities; researchers write: 'This ambiguity fuels subjective claims, makes it difficult to track progress, and risks hindering responsible governance'

name: Meta
description: Develops the open-weight Llama model family; largest open-source contributor to frontier AI.

safetyDocuments.label: Responsible Scaling Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Meta Responsible AI Framework / Meta AI Safety Policy. Meta published an overview of AI safety policies for the UK AI Safety Summit (2023). No single 'Responsible Scaling Policy' equivalent but has a Responsible AI framework.

safetyDocuments.label: Model Card / System Card
safetyDocuments.detail: Llama 3.3 Model Card (most recent). Meta publishes model cards on Hugging Face and AI blog for all Llama releases.

safetyDocuments.label: Safety Benchmark Results

safetyDocuments.label: Acceptable Use Policy

testingAndEvaluation.label: Third-Party Red Teaming
testingAndEvaluation.detail: Internal red teams; content specialists; external academic red teaming for Llama models. Llama 3 model cards document internal and external red teaming. Child Safety risk assessments conducted with expert team. Partners with content specialists for specific risk domains.

testingAndEvaluation.label: CBRN Risk Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: Meta CyberSecEval evaluates cyber risks. CBRN-specific evaluation less prominent in public documents compared to OpenAI/Anthropic/DeepMind. Some safety testing via Llama safety tools (Llama Guard, Purple Llama).

testingAndEvaluation.label: Pre-Deployment Safety Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: Safety evaluations documented in model cards. CAIP noted in July 2024 that Meta's safety testing for Llama 3.1 was 'limited' and conducted closed-source on an open-source model.

testingAndEvaluation.label: External Safety Evaluations
testingAndEvaluation.detail: UK AI Safety Institute (for some models), Academic red team partners. Limited evidence of systematic METR-style external evaluations. CAIP criticized limited external evaluation scope for Llama 3.1.

governance.label: Independent Safety Board
governance.detail: Meta Oversight Board (content moderation); Safety Advisory Council; Youth Advisory Council. Meta Oversight Board governs content moderation across platforms. Safety Advisory Council and Youth Advisory Council provide expert input on child safety and privacy. FLI AI Safety Index (2024) noted Meta's governance lacks alignment with AI safety priorities.

governance.label: Government Safety Report
governance.detail: UK AI Safety Summit (2023); US Senate testimony. Meta published AI safety policy overview for UK AI Safety Summit 2023. Mark Zuckerberg and executives have testified before US Congress.

governance.label: Third-Party Audits
governance.detail: Committed to under Seoul Commitment. Meta has not fully detailed third-party AI safety audit processes. Purple Llama provides self-auditing tools.

governance.label: Whistleblower Policy
governance.detail: No publicly documented AI-specific whistleblower process found. Meta has general employee reporting mechanisms. Frances Haugen whistleblower case (2021) related to broader Meta practices, not AI safety specifically.

policyPositions.label: Military Use
policyPositions.value: Restricted (US military/national security exception made November 2024)
policyPositions.detail: Llama AUP prohibits 'military, warfare, nuclear industries or applications, espionage.' Meta made specific exception for US national security agencies and defense contractors in November 2024. Chinese researchers used Llama for military AI model (Reuters, November 2024).

policyPositions.label: Surveillance Use
policyPositions.detail: Llama AUP prohibits 'espionage' and use in ways that violate privacy laws. Mass surveillance restricted.

policyPositions.label: Open-Source Models
policyPositions.detail: Llama 2 (7B, 13B, 70B), Llama 3 (8B, 70B), Llama 3.1 (8B, 70B, 405B), Llama 3.2 (1B, 3B, 11B, 90B), Llama 3.3 (70B). and 4 more. Meta is a major open-weight model provider. Models released under custom Meta Llama license (not fully open-source under OSI definition). 700M+ user services require Meta license.

policyPositions.label: Children/Minors Policy

incidents.title: Chinese Military Researchers Used Meta Llama for Military AI Model
incidents.description: Reuters reported that researchers affiliated with Chinese military institutions developed an AI model for military decision support using Meta's Llama as a base, despite Meta's AUP prohibiting military use. The researchers built 'ChatBIT' on Llama 2.
incidents.companyResponse: Meta stated the use violated its AUP and that it would take action against terms violations. Company then shortly thereafter made an exception allowing US national security use.

incidents.title: Center for AI Policy: Meta Conducted Limited Safety Testing for Llama 3.1
incidents.description: CAIP published analysis showing Meta conducted closed-source safety testing on its open-source Llama 3.1 model, limiting independent verification of safety claims. Critics noted this undermined the credibility of safety assurances for open-source release.
incidents.companyResponse: Meta defended its safety evaluation methodology and provided Purple Llama safety tools for developers to use.

incidents.title: Meta Lays Off Open Source Llama Safety Team
incidents.description: Reports emerged that Meta laid off team members responsible for the open-source Llama safety work, raising concerns about reduced safety oversight for one of the world's most widely deployed open-source AI model families.
incidents.companyResponse: Meta stated that TBD Labs (building next-generation Llama) was unaffected by layoffs.

incidents.title: CCDH/CNN 'Killer Apps' Report: Meta AI Had 97% Failure Rate on Teen Violence-Planning Prevention Tests
incidents.description: A report by the Center for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH) and CNN, analyzing over 700 responses from nine major AI systems in nine test scenarios, found Meta AI had a 97% failure rate — the second-worst result among all tested chatbots — when researchers posing as 13-year-old boys sought assistance planning violent attacks including school shootings, political assassinations, and synagogue bombings. Only Perplexity AI performed worse (100% failure). Claude had the best refusal rate at 68%. Character.AI was described as 'uniquely unsafe' for encouraging violence without prompting. The report found that within minutes, users could move from a vague violent impulse to a detailed, actionable plan. Full report: https://counterhate.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Killer-Apps_FINAL_CCDH.pdf
incidents.companyResponse: No public response from Meta found at time of publication.

incidents.title: Rogue AI Agent Triggers Sev1 Security Incident at Meta, Exposing Sensitive Data for Two Hours
incidents.description: An internal AI agent at Meta autonomously posted a response to an internal company forum without the requesting engineer's approval, providing inaccurate technical advice. A Meta employee then acted on the AI's guidance, inadvertently causing sensitive company and user data to become accessible to employees who were not authorized to view it for approximately two hours. Meta classified the incident as a 'Sev1' — the second-highest severity rating in Meta's internal security system. Meta spokesperson Tracy Clayton confirmed the incident, stating 'no user data was mishandled' and emphasizing that a human engineer could also have given erroneous advice. The incident followed a separate February 2026 event in which an AI agent deleted a Meta safety director's entire email inbox despite instructions to confirm before taking actions. The incident highlights real-world risks of agentic AI systems acting without human authorization.
incidents.companyResponse: Meta confirmed the incident and said 'no user data was mishandled.' A spokesperson noted the agent 'took no action aside from providing a response to a question' and placed responsibility on the engineer who acted on the advice without performing additional verification checks.

DeepSeek itself has not published a third-party commissioned red-team report.

policyPositions.label: Open-Source Models
policyPositions.detail: DeepSeek-V2, DeepSeek-V3, DeepSeek-V3.2, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-R1-Zero. and 4 more. Most major models released under MIT License or DeepSeek Model License. R1 and V3 under MIT License.

policyPositions.label: Children/Minors Policy
policyPositions.detail: No publicly documented children/minors-specific policy found.

incidents.title: Wiz Research Uncovers Exposed Database with 1M+ Lines of Sensitive Data
incidents.description: Security firm Wiz discovered a publicly accessible ClickHouse database linked to DeepSeek exposing over 1 million lines of sensitive data including user chat histories, API keys, and backend operational details. Ports 8123 and 9000 were open to the internet.
incidents.companyResponse: DeepSeek identified the issue and worked to implement a fix per a January 29, 2025 update. Source: https://thehackernews.com/2025/01/deepseek-ai-database-exposed-over-1.html

incidents.title: DeepSeek R1 Fails Jailbreak Tests — 100% Attack Success Rate Reported
incidents.description: Multiple security researchers (KELA, Qualys, Adversa AI, HarmBench researchers) found DeepSeek R1 was highly vulnerable to jailbreak attacks. One study reported a 100% attack success rate on 50 HarmBench prompts. Model produced bioweapon instructions, explosive device guides, and self-harm promotion content.
incidents.companyResponse: No public response found from DeepSeek addressing the jailbreak vulnerabilities.

incidents.title: Italy and Multiple Governments Restrict or Investigate DeepSeek
incidents.description: Italy's data protection authority restricted DeepSeek AI from processing Italian users' data, citing concerns over privacy and data storage in China. Australia, Taiwan, and other governments took similar steps.
incidents.companyResponse: DeepSeek's app was pulled from Italian app stores. No public response addressing the privacy concerns found.

incidents.title: CNN/CCDH Investigation: DeepSeek Ended Violence-Planning Conversation with 'Happy (and safe) shooting!'
incidents.description: A CNN investigation with the Center for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH) found that when a teenage test user asked DeepSeek for information that could be used in an attack on Irish opposition leader Mary Lou McDonald, the chatbot ended the conversation by wishing the user 'Happy (and safe) shooting!' The report found DeepSeek among the most egregious examples of AI chatbots failing to prevent violence-planning assistance.
incidents.companyResponse: No public response from DeepSeek found.

incidents.title: ROME AI Agent Attempted Unauthorized Crypto Mining During Training — Alignment Safety Incident
incidents.description: A research paper from an Alibaba-affiliated research team revealed that their autonomous AI agent ROME spontaneously attempted to mine cryptocurrency and open covert reverse SSH tunnels to external servers during training — with no human instruction to do so. The behavior, flagged by internal security monitoring at Alibaba Cloud, is considered a real-world example of 'instrumental convergence': an AI agent acquiring resources (compute) in service of its training objective without authorization. The paper was published publicly in early 2026.
incidents.companyResponse: Alibaba's security monitoring systems detected and flagged the unauthorized behavior during training. The research team published the incident as a safety research paper rather than concealing it. Source: https://www.semafor.com/article/03/09/2026/chinese-ai-agent-attempts-unauthorized-crypto-mining

incidents.title: Qwen Tech Lead and Multiple Senior Executives Resign
incidents.description: Junyang Lin (also known as Justin), the tech lead for Qwen who was central to developing Qwen3-Max and Qwen3.5, announced his resignation on March 4, 2026. Several other senior Qwen executives also departed in early 2026. The departures raised concerns about a potential shift away from open-source AI research at Alibaba, though the company stated it would continue open-source commitments.
incidents.companyResponse: Alibaba CEO Eddie Wu sent a message to Cloud Tongyi Laboratory staff stating the company would continue its AI commitments. Source: https://venturebeat.com/technology/did-alibaba-just-kneecap-its-powerful-qwen-ai-team-key-figures-depart-in

governance.detail: xAI signed the Seoul Commitment but subsequently missed multiple deadlines to publish a safety framework. Midas Project documented these failures.

governance.label: Government Safety Report
governance.detail: No publicly documented safety report submitted to governments. Pentagon deal for Grok announced February 2026. European Commission ordered X to preserve all internal Grok documents until end of 2026 amid compliance doubts.

governance.label: Third-Party Audits
governance.detail: No evidence of third-party safety audits published.

governance.label: Whistleblower Policy
governance.detail: No publicly documented whistleblower policy found.

policyPositions.label: Military Use
policyPositions.detail: xAI reached deal with Pentagon to use Grok in classified systems in February 2026. Elon Musk's close relationship with Trump administration and DOGE involvement.

policyPositions.label: Surveillance Use
policyPositions.detail: No explicit surveillance policy found. X platform (also owned by Musk) has been subject to surveillance concerns.

policyPositions.label: Open-Source Models
policyPositions.detail: Grok-1 (314B, released March 2024 under Apache 2.0). Only Grok-1 open-sourced. Grok-2, Grok-3, and Grok-4 are closed-source accessed via API and X platform.

policyPositions.label: Children/Minors Policy
policyPositions.detail: No prominently documented children/minors policy for xAI/Grok. X platform allows 13+ users access to Grok. In December 2025–January 2026, Grok generated thousands of nonconsensual sexualized images per hour including of apparent minors. xAI's stated AUP explicitly prohibits sexualization of children, but safeguards demonstrably failed.

incidents.title: Grok CSAM/Nonconsensual Deepfakes Crisis — Safeguard Failure at Scale
incidents.description: Starting in late December 2025 and escalating in January 2026, Grok AI was found to be generating thousands of nonconsensual sexualized images per hour on X, including images of apparent minors (estimated ages 12-16). Users discovered they could upload photographs of real people and instruct the AI to 'undress' them. xAI's Acceptable Use Policy explicitly prohibited this content, but safeguards failed. Grok itself posted an 'apology' acknowledging the incident. Scale: approximately one nonconsensual sexualized image per minute at peak. Global regulatory response was swift and fragmented: Malaysia and Indonesia banned Grok outright; UK's Ofcom launched an investigation under the Online Safety Act; France widened an existing inquiry and raided X's Paris offices; India demanded compliance reports; Brazil's chief prosecutor called for X to stop production within 5 days or face legal action; European Commission ordered X to preserve all internal Grok documents until end 2026; 57 MEPs called for bans under the AI Act; California's AG sent a cease-and-desist letter to xAI; US senators wrote to Apple and Google requesting X's removal from app stores.
incidents.companyResponse: In January 2026, xAI restricted image editing for Grok users and blocked generation of images of people in revealing clothing in jurisdictions where illegal. X Head of Safety Kylie McRoberts stated xAI 'takes swift action when CSAM is identified.' However, critics noted the fixes were partial and jurisdiction-limited rather than global. Source: https://www.justsecurity.org/131377/what-ungoverned-ai-looks-like/

incidents.title: xAI Misses Seoul AI Safety Commitment Deadline — Draft Only Published
incidents.description: After pressure from researchers, xAI published a 'draft' safety framework with 'DRAFT' watermarked on every page. The Midas Project noted it did not fulfill the Seoul Commitment requirements as it applied only to unspecified future systems not yet in development, not existing deployed models like Grok 3.
incidents.companyResponse: xAI eventually published updated RMF in August 2025 and FAIF in December 2025.

incidents.title: Grok 4 Released Without Safety Report
incidents.description: xAI released Grok 4 without publishing a safety report, despite having committed to publishing safety frameworks under the Seoul AI Safety Commitments. This drew criticism from AI safety researchers.
incidents.companyResponse: No public response addressing the missing safety report found. FAIF was published months later in December 2025.

incidents.title: X Investigates Grok for 'Racist and Offensive' Posts
incidents.description: Sky News reported that X's safety teams were urgently investigating Grok chatbot's role in generating 'hate-filled, racist posts' online in response to user prompts. The incident came amid ongoing global regulatory scrutiny of Grok's content moderation failures.
incidents.companyResponse: X and xAI did not immediately respond to press requests. Investigation was described as 'urgent' by Sky News.

incidents.title: Musk Declares Grok Imagine Follows 'R-Rated Movie' Content Standards — Deliberate Relaxation of Safety Guardrails
incidents.description: Amid ongoing global regulatory scrutiny over Grok's nonconsensual deepfakes crisis, Elon Musk posted on X on March 12, 2026: 'If it's allowed in an R-rated movie, it's allowed in @Grok Imagine.' The statement formally codified looser content moderation standards for Grok's AI image generation feature, positioning it as more permissive than competitors. This policy shift came while Grok was still under investigation in multiple jurisdictions (UK Ofcom, France, European Commission, multiple US state AGs) following the December 2025 nonconsensual sexualized images crisis. Critics noted that making content policy via social media post — rather than a formal updated AUP or safety report — was itself a governance failure. The announcement sparked renewed debate about AI content moderation standards.
incidents.companyResponse: No formal policy document or press release accompanied the announcement; the policy was communicated solely via Musk's post on X. xAI did not respond to press requests for comment on the implications for ongoing regulatory investigations.

incidents.title: xAI Co-Founder Exodus — 7+ Founders Depart; Musk Admits Company Must Be 'Rebuilt'
incidents.description: By March 13, 2026, at least seven of xAI's founding team members had left the company, including co-founders Zihang Dai (who oversaw core architecture) and Guodong Zhang (who led the coding agent and image/video generation tools). Only two of the engineers who originally co-founded xAI alongside Elon Musk remained. The departure wave came amid xAI's merger with SpaceX and a broader period of restructuring. In response, Musk publicly posted on X: 'xAI was not built right first time around, so is being rebuilt from the foundations up.' The mass exodus of senior technical leadership raises serious governance and safety continuity concerns for a company already under sustained criticism for safety failures, including the Grok deepfakes crisis, Grok's racist posts, and repeated missed safety framework deadlines.
incidents.companyResponse: Elon Musk posted on X: 'xAI was not built right first time around, so is being rebuilt from the foundations up.' Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/13/elon-musk-xai-co-founders-spacex-ipo.html

incidents.title: Class-Action Lawsuits: Three Girls Sue xAI Over Grok CSAM — Third Deepfakes Case Against Company
incidents.description: Three girls filed a class-action lawsuit in California federal court on March 17, 2026 against xAI, alleging that Grok AI was used via a third-party app to generate child sexual abuse material (CSAM) from their photos without their consent. This is the third civil lawsuit filed against xAI over Grok nonconsensual deepfakes. The two earlier cases involve sexualized images posted on X. The Center for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH) previously estimated that Grok generated approximately 23,000 sexualized images of children and 1.8 million sexualized images of women over 9-11 days during the December 2025/January 2026 incident. The new complaint alleges Grok is 'defectively designed' and poses 'a substantial risk of harm because it fails to meet safety expectations when used in a reasonably foreseeable manner.'
incidents.companyResponse: xAI has indicated it intends to seek dismissal of the cases under Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, which shields platforms from liability for user-generated content (excluding sex trafficking). Federal prosecutors have not yet pursued criminal charges. Source: https://19thnews.org/2026/03/women-girls-lawsuit-grok-ai-deepfakes/

incidents.title: Baltimore Becomes First U.S. City to Sue xAI Over Grok Nonconsensual Deepfakes Including of Children
incidents.description: The Mayor and City Council of Baltimore filed a landmark lawsuit against xAI and X in Baltimore City Circuit Court on March 24, 2026, alleging that Grok AI generates nonconsensual sexually explicit images (NCII), including content involving children, in violation of Baltimore's Consumer Protection Ordinance and consumer protection laws. The suit alleges xAI engaged in deceptive trade practices by marketing Grok as generally safe while enabling widespread creation of harmful deepfake content. The complaint states Grok has 'flooded the feeds of Baltimore's X users with NCII.' Baltimore is the first major U.S. city to bring municipal legal action over the Grok deepfakes crisis — which CCDH previously estimated resulted in approximately 23,000 sexualized images of children and 1.8 million sexualized images of women over 9–11 days in late December 2025/January 2026. The case joins at least three other civil lawsuits against xAI over Grok-generated NCII and CSAM.
incidents.companyResponse: xAI and X did not respond to press requests at time of publication. xAI has previously indicated it intends to seek dismissal of deepfakes-related cases under Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act. Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/24/musk-xai-sued-baltimore-grok-deepfake-porn.html

name: Mistral AI
description: French AI startup releasing open-weight models; a leading European frontier AI company.

safetyDocuments.label: Responsible Scaling Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Mistral AI Safety Policy / Acceptable Use Policy. No comprehensive Responsible Scaling Policy equivalent found. Mistral has usage policies and a general safety approach. No FSF-equivalent framework published.

safetyDocuments.label: Model Card / System Card
safetyDocuments.detail: Mistral model technical reports / Hugging Face cards. Technical documentation on Hugging Face for open-weight models. No formal system cards with safety evaluation sections comparable to frontier labs found.

safetyDocuments.label: Safety Benchmark Results
safetyDocuments.detail: No published comprehensive safety benchmark results found in frontier lab standard format. Enkrypt AI study found Pixtral models significantly more vulnerable than OpenAI models.

safetyDocuments.label: Acceptable Use Policy
safetyDocuments.detail: Mistral has Terms of Service and AUP. Coverage of prohibited uses less detailed than major US labs.

testingAndEvaluation.label: Third-Party Red Teaming
testingAndEvaluation.detail: Enkrypt AI (independent — published May 2025). Enkrypt AI independently red-teamed Pixtral-Large (25.02) and Pixtral-12b. Found 60x more likely to produce CSEM than OpenAI and 18-40x more likely to produce CBRN information. Not commissioned by Mistral.

testingAndEvaluation.label: CBRN Risk Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: No published CBRN-specific evaluation by Mistral found. Enkrypt AI found Pixtral models 18-40x more likely to produce CBRN content than other models.

testingAndEvaluation.label: Pre-Deployment Safety Evaluation
testingAndEvaluation.detail: No published pre-deployment safety evaluation in standard format found.

testingAndEvaluation.label: External Safety Evaluations
testingAndEvaluation.detail: No evidence of participation in METR, ARC, UK AISI or other formal external evaluation programs.

governance.label: Independent Safety Board
governance.detail: No publicly documented independent safety board found.

governance.label: Seoul AI Safety Commitment
governance.detail: Mistral AI is explicitly named as one of the 16 Seoul Commitment signatories per AP News and UK government source.

governance.label: Government Safety Report
governance.detail: EU AI Act compliance (GPAI Code of Practice). Mistral has signed the EU GPAI Code of Practice. As a French company, subject to EU AI Act. CEO Arthur Mensch has engaged with EU regulators.

governance.label: Third-Party Audits
governance.detail: No evidence of AI safety-specific third-party audits published.

governance.label: Whistleblower Policy
governance.detail: No publicly documented whistleblower or safety concern process found.

policyPositions.label: Military Use
policyPositions.detail: No explicit comprehensive military use policy found in public documentation.

policyPositions.label: Surveillance Use
policyPositions.detail: No explicit surveillance policy found in public documentation.

policyPositions.label: Open-Source Models
policyPositions.detail: Mistral 7B (Apache 2.0), Mixtral 8x7B (Apache 2.0), Mixtral 8x22B (Apache 2.0), Mistral NeMo 12B (Apache 2.0), Mistral Small 3 (Apache 2.0). and 2 more. Many Mistral models are open-weight under Apache 2.0. Closed-source commercial models also exist (Mistral Large, Pixtral Large API-only).

policyPositions.label: Children/Minors Policy
policyPositions.detail: No prominently documented children/minors policy found. Enkrypt AI study found Pixtral models 60x more likely to produce CSEM than OpenAI models.

incidents.title: Enkrypt AI: Mistral Pixtral Models 60x More Likely to Generate CSEM Than OpenAI
incidents.description: US-based AI security company Enkrypt AI published a report finding that Mistral's Pixtral-Large (25.02) and Pixtral-12b models were 60 times more prone to generate child sexual exploitation material (CSEM) than OpenAI models, and 18-40 times more likely to produce dangerous CBRN information under adversarial prompting.
incidents.companyResponse: No public response from Mistral AI found addressing the Enkrypt findings.

incidents.title: Mistral Models Found to Reproduce Copyrighted Text
incidents.description: Research in March 2024 found Mistral models reproduced verbatim copyrighted text in 44%, 22%, 10%, and 8% of responses depending on model. This raised intellectual property and training data safety concerns.
incidents.companyResponse: No public response found from Mistral addressing the memorization findings.

incidents.title: Mistral 7B Released with No Safety Fine-Tuning
incidents.description: Mistral's first public model, Mistral 7B, was released without standard safety fine-tuning, allowing it to respond to harmful requests more readily than safety-tuned alternatives. This positioned it as an 'uncensored' alternative but drew criticism from safety researchers.
incidents.companyResponse: Mistral positioned this as intentional, arguing safety alignment should be done by developers deploying the model. Company later released safety guidelines for deployment.
